reasoning: The market probability was 0.06 in mid-October. Since then, no major escalations have occurred that would point to an imminent closure. Historically, Iran has frequently threatened to close the Strait of Hormuz but never followed through, due to the severe economic self-harm it would cause and the likelihood of angering its main customer, China. Significantly, even after direct US airstrikes on Iranian nuclear sites in June 2025 and the E3's triggering of UN snapback sanctions in August/September 2025, Iran restricted itself to parliamentary threats (a June vote to close the strait) and low-level harassment, without enacting an actual blockade or severe restriction of Western shipping. Given that these massive provocations failed to trigger a closure, and with only two months remaining in 2025, the probability of them suddenly doing so before the end of the year is very low. A 5% chance appropriately reflects the remaining tail risk of abrupt escalation out of an already highly volatile environment.
